summary: My parents came from Fukui Prefecture, Japan. They immigrated to the United States around 1920 and settled first in San Francisco before moving to Sacramento County. Their roots in Fukui were important to our family identity, even as we built a new life here in America.

My parents first came to San Francisco when they immigrated from Japan around 1920. It was their initial landing place in America before they moved inland to Sacramento County to farm. San Francisco was the starting point of their new life in the United States.

After arriving in San Francisco, my parents moved to Sacramento County where they became farmers. I was born in Florin, a small town in Sacramento County, and grew up there before the war. Our family worked the land and lived in a mostly Japanese community in that area.

I was born and raised in Florin, a little town out in the country in Sacramento County. Before the war, life there was centered around farming and our Japanese community. I have fond memories of going to Japanese movies in Florin and playing samurai sword fights with neighborhood kids.

Elk Grove was the place where we were evacuated from during the war. My sister drove us there to the train station when we were forced to leave our home. It was a sad day leaving behind our dog and our possessions as we boarded the train to the assembly center.

I was about nine or ten years old when Pearl Harbor was attacked in December 1941. I remember listening to the radio with my family when the news broke. My brother, who was in the United States Army, had to return to duty immediately after the attack.

My brother served in the United States Army and was visiting us when Pearl Harbor was attacked. After the attack, he was called back to active duty. The Army was a significant part of our family’s experience during the war.

Executive Order 9066 forced us to evacuate from our home and go to the assembly center. It was a sudden and forced removal, and we had to sell almost everything we owned. My family and many others were uprooted because of this government order.

Before we were sent to camp, my parents heard that the FBI might come to our home. My mother burned her picture of Emperor Hirohito and other belongings from Japan to avoid trouble. My father buried his samurai sword and my brother buried a revolver rather than turn them over to the FBI.

My mother had a picture of Emperor Hirohito from Japan, but when the FBI was expected to come, she burned it along with other Japanese items. This was a difficult time, and my family tried to avoid any trouble by getting rid of anything that might be seen as suspicious.

My father was an instructor of kenbu, a Japanese sword dance. Even in the camp, he taught kenbu using a wooden sword since he couldn’t have his real one. It was a way for him to keep connected to his culture and to stay active during those difficult times.

Along with kenbu, my father incorporated shigin, a form of Japanese singing, into his teaching at the camp. This helped maintain cultural traditions while we were interned. It was important for him to keep those practices alive despite the circumstances.

Elk Grove was the town where we boarded the train to go to the assembly center. My sister drove us there, and it was a sad moment leaving our dog behind. Elk Grove marked the beginning of our forced journey into internment.

We were sent to the Fresno Assembly Center first, where we stayed for about four months. It was like a county fairground, cleaner and better than some other places where people were kept in horse stalls. Even though it was a prison with barbed wire and guards, the conditions were relatively decent and they took care of our health and schooling.

I have a younger sister who still lives in Gilroy, California. After the war and all the upheaval, some of my family members settled in different places like Gilroy and Sacramento. It’s where part of my family’s story continues today.

One of my sisters-in-law lives in Sacramento, California. Our family had strong ties to the Sacramento area since my parents farmed there before the war. Sacramento remains an important place for our family connections.

We entered the internment camp right after the Great Depression. Coming from a poor family, we were used to eating rice morning, noon, and night, and bread was a luxury we rarely had. The camp provided three meals a day and a roof over our heads, which was a different experience from the hardships we faced during the Depression.

After leaving Fresno, we were sent to the Jerome War Relocation Center. The train ride took four days and nights, and we stopped in New Mexico where locals thought we were Native Americans because of our appearance. Jerome was part of the long journey and experience of internment during the war.

During our transfer to the Jerome War Relocation Center, the train stopped in New Mexico. The people there asked us what tribe we were from because they thought we were Native Americans due to our high cheekbones. It was a surprising and memorable moment on our long journey.
